data.title: Should the Hong Kong stock exchange make all IPO applications confidential?
data.description:
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) last week proposed its biggest set of listing reforms since 2018 for Asia’s third-largest stock exchange. Delivered in a consultation paper, the reforms aim to further improve the competitiveness of the exchange that topped the world rankings for initial public offerings (IPOs) last year.
The bourse operator will collect views on the proposals, which include broadening a special listing regime for innovative companies and opening the door for listings by smaller and more diverse firms, until May 8.
Investment banks and brokers have piped up in support of a proposal to allow all companies that are pursuing listings to file their applications confidentially – a privilege currently given only to specific types of companies. Market participants believe the reform would attract more listings and enhance Hong Kong as an international financial centre because many other exchanges around the world already keep applications confidential.
Here is what you need to know about how the change would affect the market.
What is the current situation?
Since October 2013, HKEX has required all listing candidates to publish their listing documents, including corporate history, business models and financial information, on the HKEX website once they submit the application to the exchange.
The requirement aims to enhance market transparency by providing more information for investors and research analysts at the early stage of the listing process.
It also aims to improve the quality of listing documents, as listing sponsors are expected to step up their efforts to ensure public documents are up to standard.
The current rules allow confidential filing only for overseas-listed companies seeking a secondary listing, certain biotechnological firms and other technology applicants.
Those listing candidates can – and all candidates under the proposal could – give listing documents to the exchange for private vetting upon application submission, only publishing the information online after listing approval.
Why consider a change?
“The key benefit is the removal of a significant deterrent for high-quality companies,” said Rocky Tung Yat-ngok, executive director of the Financial Services Development Council, a government think tank.
“Currently, mandatory immediate disclosure exposes commercially sensitive information, including business strategies, to competitors during the regulatory process. Confidential filing protects companies during this sensitive phase, making Hong Kong a more attractive listing destination for potential issuers.”

HKEX head of listing Katherine Ng said the proposal for confidential filing by all candidates would ensure equal treatment for companies and bring Hong Kong in line with practices in other major markets.
What other markets allow confidential filing?
The stock markets in the UK and Singapore have no publication requirements for draft prospectuses upon submission of listing applications. The US had expanded confidential filing to all candidates in recent years, according to the HKEX consultation paper.
“Many overseas major markets, such as the stock exchanges in the US and UK, allow listing candidates to have confidential filing,” said John Lee Chen-kwok, vice-chairman and co-head of Asia coverage at UBS in Hong Kong.
“This will enhance the appeal and competitiveness of the Hong Kong stock markets to listing candidates, particularly those in innovative industries, as early disclosure of their technology and business operation details may affect their competitiveness and business operations.”
Hong Kong lawmaker Robert Lee Wai-wang, chairman of Grand Finance Group, said he supported the rule change, noting that the gap between filing an application and receiving approval may be several months.
“The current rule requires companies to disclose a lot of sensitive information for several months before they can get approval for listing,” he said. “In some cases, some companies may have disclosed all this information, but eventually they cannot get the approval.”
That could discourage good candidates from listing in Hong Kong, leading them to opt for the US or London, he added.
“It is fair to the companies to make the disclosure only after they have obtained the approval for listing.”
What about transparency concerns?
Ng said the change would not exempt companies from providing information to investors; it would just change the timing.
“The exchange would still allow companies to opt for publication of their listing documents once they apply for listing,” she added.

Will the change affect the quality of listing documents?
In December, market regulator the Securities and Futures Commission and HKEX ordered 13 investment banks to review their procedures and staffing after finding widespread deficiencies in IPO submissions. The regulator said on Thursday that it would soon begin inspections of investment banks to make sure they were meeting expectations.
HKEX’s listing reform proposal includes an enhanced return mechanism as a powerful safeguard to ensure accountability and deter poor-quality filings.
Under the proposal, HKEX would return substandard documents and identify all responsible professional parties – sponsors, lawyers and accountants – to the public.
“We believe it strikes the right balance of protecting commercial confidentiality early on to attract more issuers, while ensuring the public receives full, vetted information before any investment decision,” the HKEX paper said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s ‘ghost oil’ stations rake in ‘HK$10,000 a day’ as petrol prices soar
data.description:
Operators of illicit fuel stations can earn as much as HK$10,000 (US$1,280) a day by smuggling untaxed petrol into Hong Kong using vehicles equipped with enlarged tanks and selling it to car club members via coded messages, the South China Morning Post has learned.
A source said there had been more cases involving illicit fuel or “ghost oil” in the city since late February, when petrol prices began to soar following US-Israel strikes on Iran.
The source said law enforcement raids usually discovered trucks equipped with enlarged tanks filled with untaxed fuel from mainland China, vehicles acting as mobile stations carrying untaxed petrol, and fixed illicit filling stations.
Fixed stations were usually hidden in sheds in remote areas, the source said. Operators would offload the illicit fuel from their vehicles into barrels at the site for patrons or fill up other vehicles acting as mobile stations elsewhere.
“There are transit points for the fuel to be distributed to other areas,” the source said.
Illicit filling stations could earn up to HK$10,000 per day, according to the source, as they profited from price differences between local taxed petrol and lower-priced options on the mainland.
“Take 95-octane petrol as an example, it costs around 8 yuan [US$1.16] on the mainland, which is around HK$9. In Hong Kong, petrol costs around HK$30 a litre, maybe around HK$25 to HK$26 with a discount included,” the source said.
Hong Kong does not sell 95-octane petrol, a cheaper variety that is widely available on the mainland and in other countries. Local fuel stations offer 98-octane unleaded petrol instead, which is more expensive.

Consumer Council data showed that Friday’s pump price for standard petrol stood at HK$31.09 a litre, while the net price after a walk-in discount ranged between HK$23.09 and HK$30.19.
The source said illicit vendors would earn a tidy profit even by selling their fuel HK$2 or HK$3 above the mainland price.
“For a station that stocks 1,000 to 2,000 litres, the profit will be in the thousands, with revenue up to HK$10,000 a day,” the source said.
Toyota Estima owner Jack Chan*, 39, has been using illegal fuel for seven years.
“Many drivers join car clubs, and these clubs have WhatsApp groups. Usually, the sources for ‘ghost oil’ are found within these groups,” Chan, an engineer, said. “We don’t explicitly say we want ‘ghost oil’; we use the code ‘I want to drink milk’ instead.”
Chan said fuel providers would message him upon seeing the code words to exchange contact details. The supplier and Chan would then meet at a remote location, usually in Yuen Long, where Chan lives.


Filling the 60-litre tank in his car with illicit petrol, Chan can save up to HK$600. He pays about HK$800 to HK$900 to fill his tank with “ghost oil”, while the legal option would cost between HK$1,300 and HK$1,500.
Chan said suppliers drove modified seven-seaters, with the rear seats removed to make room for a large fuel tank.
“They come equipped with their own fuel nozzles to pump the petrol into my car,” he said. “The nozzle has an electronic meter, so it’s all digitised. I can clearly see how much fuel I have taken, and then we settle the transaction in cash.”
Authorities have also focused on clamping down on illegal fuel stations amid the price surge. A joint crackdown by the Customs and Excise Department and the Fire Services Department on three illegal fuelling stations this week resulted in two people being arrested and the seizure of more than 1,800 litres of petrol estimated to be worth HK$54,000.
A spokeswoman for customs said 18 illicit fuel operations had been smashed as of February. Officers seized 19,974 litres of illegal fuel and 12 vehicles, and arrested 17 people.
“Customs will continue to work closely with relevant departments, conduct joint operations when appropriate to holistically tackle illegal fuelling activities. We will also apply to the court to confiscate the vehicles involved,” the spokeswoman said.

The Fire Services Department said that it received 221 complaints about illegal petrol stations in the first two months of this year, or about 111 per month – 42 per cent higher than the monthly average of 78 cases in 2025.
The department seized 12,763 litres of petrol and 180,454 litres of diesel this year as of February 28, instituting 76 prosecutions.
Ringo Lee Yiu-pui, governor and honorary life president of the Hong Kong, China Automobile Association, said substandard fuel used to be sold illegally at lower prices.
However, the supply of this type of poor-quality illegal fuel was reduced after the government removed duties for Euro V diesel in 2008.
“Illicit filling stations could simply use the proper fuel [bought elsewhere]. That was in a sense legal as there were no duties, but illegal stations were still illegal for violating fire safety laws,” Lee said.
However, petrol is taxed in the city, rendering sales beyond established filling stations illegal.
Lee said while most illegal filling stations supplied diesel, a cheaper fuel widely used by commercial vehicles, he had noticed an uptick in those providing petrol recently.
“If the illicit station provides petrol, it’s more dangerous as the fuel is more volatile than diesel,” Lee warned.
Lee urged the government to introduce balance mechanisms to regulate fuel prices to reduce demand for cheaper illegal alternatives.
*The interviewee’s name has been changed.

data.title: Hong Kong police hunt 3 suspects over US$12 million gold theft
data.description:
Hong Kong police have launched a manhunt for three people involved in a suspected deception case in which a shop reportedly lost 73kg of gold bars worth an estimated US$12 million, the South China Morning Post has learned.
The force said on Friday that a woman in Hung Hom had made a report around 2.57pm that a nearby shop was suspected to have been “burgled”. The woman said the shop owner had told her someone had stolen the gold bars before fleeing in a private vehicle.
A source said the case involved a privately owned gold shop located in the Peninsula Square commercial building on Sung On Street.
A former partner of the shop allegedly tricked the manager into handing over gold bars worth around US$12 million, the source said. The man then got into a black Tesla with his wife and a male driver and fled the scene.
The force said that during the investigation officers pulled over a white vehicle, but it was later determined to be unrelated to the case.
The car was reportedly stopped near a toll plaza on the North Lantau Highway on the way to the airport.
The source said the case had been classified as “obtaining property by deception”.

data.title: Residents of Tai Po blaze building misled over renovation project, inquiry told
data.description:
Residents of a housing complex destroyed in one of Hong Kong’s deadliest fires were misled into picking a convicted contractor for the estate’s renovation project amid “widespread” bid-rigging and other corruption in the construction industry, a public hearing has been told.
The judge-led independent committee also heard on Friday that an engineering consultant allegedly accepted a monthly stipend to “rubber stamp” tender evaluation reports, including one recommending Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po pick the most expensive option offered by a contractor .
In his opening speech on the second day of a series of evidential hearings, the committee’s leading senior counsel, Victor Dawes, highlighted what appeared to be suspicious connections between the contractor and five of its competitors in the tendering process, suggesting they could have “some forms of relationship” under the table.
Dawes said various law enforcement agencies shared the view that bid-rigging and other anticompetitive practices were rife in the city, with police noting that people with triad backgrounds had set up engineering and construction firms hoping to monopolise the building renovation business.
“The information received from law enforcement is consistent, that such practice is widespread and common in the market,” he said.

The inferno that tore through seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks in November last year was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948, killing 168 people and leaving nearly 5,000 homeless.
All eight buildings had been under renovation and covered with bamboo scaffolding and mesh netting since the summer of 2024.
The HK$336 million (US$42.89 million) renovation contract for Prestige Construction and Engineering Company, the priciest among 57 proposals offered, was passed under unusual circumstances earlier that year, as 570 votes were reportedly cast despite only 293 residents, including those represented by authorised people, being present.
The then chairman of the estate’s management committee later ignored residents’ requests to convene a meeting to overturn the decision.
In 2021, residents chose Will Power Architects Company as the project’s consultant after it offered to conduct an initial inspection of the estate and to oversee the work for HK$220,000 and HK$308,000 respectively, well below the market rate
In an evaluation report in 2024, Will Power picked Prestige from 57 contractors as its “first choice”, highlighting its “clear” criminal record over the preceding eight years.
Dawes said the consultant’s director, Wilson Ng Yeuk, who claimed to have approved the report, had likely not reviewed the bids at all.

The barrister said Prestige was convicted 24 times for occupational safety breaches between 2017 and 2023.
After a Will Power employee noticed the report had been amended to give Prestige a high rating for its “clear” record – a change that ultimately placed the contractor above the rest in the final ranking – another company director, Wong Hap-yin, told him to continue using the modified version.
“We believe residents had already been misled at this stage,” Dawes said, adding that Wong could have deliberately falsified the document.
The evidence also suggested that Ng, who was employed by Swire Properties at the time of the fire, had admitted to working as a “freelancer” for Will Power for a monthly stipend of HK$15,000 to act as a registered inspector, earning a total of HK$190,000.
“He could very likely have simply been a rubber stamp, putting his signature to whatever document was given to him,” Dawes added.




Ng sold all his shares at Will Power to Wong for HK$1 on the evening of November 26, hours after the fire began.
The apparent interconnections between Prestige and five other competitors in the bidding process have also come under scrutiny.
Prestige’s director, Hau Wa-kin, jointly owned a company called Rich Surplus Develop with Richie Tam Chee-man, who was the director of competitor Man Sing General Contractors.
Leung Ping-yiu, the director of Widely Construction & Engineering, was formerly a director of Prestige.
Hau also owned a now-dissolved company, Blazing Edge, together with Li Chik-hoi and Choi Man-ming, who were directors of San Hoi Ming Construction Engineering and Sunarts (Holdings) Engineering respectively. Li’s and Choi’s companies had also bid for the Wang Fuk Court project.
Blazing Edge had shared the same company secretary as Mega Praise Construction & Engineering, another bidder.
Dawes said Hau, who declined to give live evidence at the hearings, had failed to give satisfactory answers to the committee’s queries.
The rapid spread of the fire and the high death toll could be partly due to the use of polyfoam boards to cover windows, replacement of fireproof windows with wooden doors in evacuation passages and the failure of fire safety systems, the committee heard.
Dawes said the use of combustible boards had triggered repeated complaints from residents, but the Fire Services Department and the Housing Bureau’s Independent Checking Unit both believed the concerns fell outside their area of supervision.
Prestige purchased a new set of polyfoam boards in 2024 to address the complaints.
Text messages in October 2024 revealed that Tony Tsui moon-come, then chairman of the estate’s management committee, told Prestige director Gordon Ho Kin-yip to find a sample that was “not so flammable” to conduct a fire test and show the result to residents, adding a video to be taken should not be “too fake”.
Ho replied the first batch of boards would not be replaced.
Dawes said the exchange, while incomplete, at least showed the pair’s understanding that the contractor might have purchased substandard items and existing flammable boards would not be removed.
The barrister also took the Independent Checking Unit to task for failing to make an on-site inspection of the illegal alterations to Wang Fuk Court’s evacuation staircases.
Dawes pointed out that replacing some windows with wooden doors had allowed toxic gas from outside to spread into homes during the fire.
He also said the estate’s fire safety contractor, China Status Development and Engineering, had failed to assess the necessity of shutting down the fire hosepipes and alarms for more than half a year, leading to the almost complete failure of fire safety measures when the blaze broke out.
Tsui also gave a statement on Friday, saying he and other management committee members had been subject to unfair criticisms after the fire and he was confident the committee could “restore the truth”.
“I appreciate the [independent] committee’s effort to compile such a large sum of information and restore the truth,” he said tearfully.
“I know that there are many misunderstandings about us from the public, and I am grateful to the committee for allowing us to speak out about what we know.”

Outside the hearing, Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han said the Labour Department would fully cooperate with the probe, after the first session on Thursday revealed that no action had been taken against smoking workers after 16 site checks despite residents’ repeated complaints.
“If there is a need for accountability, it will be pursued to the very end,” Sun said.
Legal counsel for the government will deliver an opening statement when the hearings resume at City Gallery in Central on Tuesday. The first witness may testify that afternoon.

data.title: Swire Coca-Cola wins HK$32 million government deal after bottled water scandal
data.description:
Swire Coca-Cola has secured a HK$31.9 million (US$4 million) contract to supply Bonaqua water to some Hong Kong government offices, following a HK$52.9 million bottled water procurement scandal last year.
According to a Government Logistics Department notice, Swire Coca-Cola was awarded the 27-month contract on Tuesday to supply more than 1.46 million bottles of drinking water to offices on Hong Kong Island and parts of the outlying islands. The selection followed an open tender launched in December.
In its tender documents, the department added several requirements, including requiring suppliers to prove they held a market share over the past 10 years, had a minimum of 50 sales channels, and possessed a daily order delivery capacity of at least 100 orders.
If the bottled water is imported, the supplier must meet the qualifications of a food importer and submit a safety test report. If the total bid price exceeds HK$15 million, bidders must pass a financial review, pay a contract deposit and allow authorities to conduct an on-site inspection.
In June 2023, Swire Coca-Cola also won a bid to supply bottled water involving 1.35 million bottles of drinking water for HK$40.5 million.
The government came under scrutiny last August over a three-year HK$52.9 million bottled water supply contract awarded to Xin Ding Xin Trade. It was the first time mainland Chinese suppliers won the contract, supposedly saving the city’s 170,000 civil servants an average of about HK$16.2 million a year on bottled water supplies.
The company allegedly submitted fraudulent documents and is suspected of defrauding the department and breaching the Trade Descriptions Ordinance.
Following the scandal, authorities promptly terminated the contract and said they would pick a new supplier of bottled drinking water for government offices on Hong Kong Island and some outlying islands through open tendering.

Before the new contract took effect, the department arranged for Watson Group to temporarily supply bottled drinking water to government offices on Hong Kong Island and some outlying islands for a period of four months, amounting to about HK$7.1 million.
The Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau said the government’s procurement process would involve strengthened requirements, with minister Christopher Hui Ching-yu chairing a task force set up in the wake of the scandal in August, to review the procurement process.
They include stipulating specific due diligence requirements, expanding the roles of the logistics department’s audit team and revising standard government contracts to empower authorities to conduct site inspections during the vetting process.
After the scandal emerged, a separate government audit review released in October last year revealed that the logistics department had repeatedly ignored signs of fraud – including an incorrect name on the company chop – during the vetting process and in the initial stage of contract operations.
This oversight allowed the company, accused of allegedly using falsified water quality test certificates, to win the government tender and supply substandard products.
A police investigation found that Xin Ding Xin Trade sourced water from a Dongguan plant instead of the Guangzhou supplier named in its contract.
The force also discovered the company had no connection with the Guangzhou company it claimed to be sourcing water from.
Instead, the firm used a water quality certificate provided by the Guangzhou plant to meet tender requirements, without the plant’s knowledge.
The scandal last year also prompted the government to rescind the head of the logistics department Carlson Chan Ka-shun’s Silver Bauhinia Star nomination.

data.title: 13 arrested over HK$113 million laundered using government’s ‘iAM Smart’ app
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested 13 people on suspicion of laundering more than HK$113 million (US$14.4 million) using the government’s “iAM Smart” app in scams run by a syndicate.
The force said on Friday that nine men and four women, aged between 38 and 75, were suspected of using victims’ “iAM Smart” accounts to launder money through “mule” bank accounts.
“The syndicate placed fake job advertisements in newspapers, claiming to be hiring cleaners,” said Wong Yuk-yan, acting superintendent from the New Territories North division.
“They then accompanied these jobseekers to open ‘iAM Smart’ accounts at self-registration kiosks and used their accounts for payment and identity verification to collect their proceeds from scams.”
The syndicate laundered a total of HK$113 million between June 2025 and this January through this method.
Senior Inspector Fung Tat-yan, from the New Territories North regional technology and financial crime unit, said the force noticed a rise in fraud cases involving the use of savings and payment accounts as mule accounts towards the end of last year.
Fung said the syndicate opened basic stored value payment accounts using different phone numbers and asked victims to log into the “iAM Smart” apps on mobile phones they had prepared to link these accounts using their identities.
“The fraudsters told jobseekers that their ‘iAM Smart’ accounts were needed for work registration and future pay cheques,” he said.

They misled victims using different reasons, deceiving them into believing they needed to register for job certification and provide their fingerprints and photos, exploiting their personal information, Fung said.
The syndicate then used these accounts to launder money from scams and deposit fraudulent proceeds, he added.
He said the suspects operated in an organised manner, with some responsible for placing false advertisements while others accompanied victims to the “iAM Smart” self-registration kiosk.
Checks by the South China Morning Post found nearly 100 “iAM Smart” self-registration kiosks across the city.
Police analysed fraud cases and gathered intelligence before arresting the 13 suspects, who claimed to be cleaners, construction workers, waiters and unemployed, with some having a triad background. They are currently out on bail and pending investigation.
Wong reminded members of the public that “iAM Smart” is a form of digital identification used for more than 1,400 public and private online services, and warned against lending their accounts to others.
Together with 12 related arrests made since last October, the force has arrested 25 people allegedly involved in the syndicate and uncovered 105 scam cases, primarily involving online jobs, with losses amounting to HK$8 million.

data.title: Hong Kong should use ‘safe haven’ status to draw capital from Gulf: agency chief
data.description:
Hong Kong should leverage its position as a “safe haven for investment” to attract foreign capital amid the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, the head of InvestHK has said, noting that more companies using Dubai as a hub have shifted to the city to diversify risk.
Alpha Lau Hai-suen, director general of the Hong Kong government’s investment promotion agency, said on Friday that businesses were cautious about expanding operations, given the rising operational costs and uncertainty stemming from the US-Israel war on Iran and Tehran’s attacks on infrastructure in Dubai, United Arab Emirates.
Lau said Hong Kong should seize the opportunity as a safe haven to draw foreign capital based in Dubai, even as the government there continued to offer tax incentives, preferential treatment and financial support to attract international investors.
She noted that China and other Asian countries with strong economic growth were some of the top destinations for companies seeking expansion, while sovereign funds had also shown an interest in diverting investment to the city and the East, even before the US-Israel war on Iran began last month.

“What we have seen is that not only Middle Eastern money, but companies and countries that have traditionally used Dubai as a hub, have now shifted mostly to Hong Kong – maybe a little bit to Singapore – because they want to diversify their risk,” Lau said in an interview on the sidelines of a seminar co-hosted by InvestHK in Beijing.
“[We] have also seen more interest in investments in the Middle East – whether it’s European or Middle Eastern funds – to move into Asia. So we can seize upon this.”
Lau said InvestHK had also received inquiries from high-net-worth individuals in the Middle East who wished to place their capital in Hong Kong and mainland China, which they viewed as safe destinations.
She said Hong Kong could play a key role in helping mainland companies go global as it was an international city with a free economy and was attracting more overseas-based talent.
Lau added that mainland companies could set up offshore management and financial centres in Hong Kong to manage their risks.
Looking ahead, Lau said InvestHK would focus more on building ties with new markets while making precise efforts to lure investment from traditional markets, such as seeking opportunities in Europe for green and life sciences technology.
She noted that the Global South was an important market, including Southeast Asian countries, while more mainland companies were also seeking opportunities in Central Asia and South America.


Secretary for Commerce and Economic Development Algernon Yau Ying-wah told the seminar that Hong Kong served as a key platform for connecting mainland companies with international markets and as an important gateway for foreign firms entering the Chinese market.
He said Hong Kong offered a stable investment environment and predictable policies for global investors, thanks to the country’s support and the city’s own strengths.
Yau highlighted that the city was highly internationalised, with a comprehensive legal framework, a simple tax regime and the free flow of capital, people, resources and data.
He noted that more than 11,000 mainland and foreign firms had set up offices in the city last year, with over 1,500 of them using Hong Kong as their regional headquarters.
Mainland companies topped the list, with more than 3,000 of them setting up in the city, marking a year-on-year increase of 18 per cent, he added.
At the seminar, InvestHK and the China Council for International Investment Promotion also signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) to promote and support mainland enterprises expanding overseas.
The MOU aims to strengthen exchanges, cooperation and promotion in aspects such as two-way investment, international market exploration, talent exchange, and economic and trade activities.

data.title: ByteDance sells games studio Moonton to Saudi firm for over US$6 billion
data.description:
ByteDance has struck a deal to sell its gaming studio Moonton to Savvy Games Group, a Saudi gaming company owned by the country’s sovereign wealth fund, for more than US$6 billion, the two sides said on Friday.
Moonton, known for the popular online battle title Mobile Legends: Bang Bang, recently signed an agreement to sell to Savvy, owned by Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund, Moonton CEO Zhang Yunfan said in an internal memo on Thursday seen by the South China Morning Post.
A Savvy representative said in an email to the SCMP that the deal was worth more than US$6 billion, adding that the company would complete the acquisition “over the coming months”. ByteDance acquired Moonton in 2021 at a valuation of around US$4 billion, Reuters reported.
Both sides said that Zhang would stay on as Moonton’s CEO, and the management team would remain unchanged. Zhang said in the memo that the studio would continue to be based in Shanghai.

Upon completion of the transaction, the firm would provide employees with incentives, including “accelerated vesting and payout of certain long-term incentives granted in the past”, Zhang said.
Moonton’s sale is another retreat from gaming for ByteDance, which is pivoting to AI. In 2024, the TikTok owner sold two gaming units to Tencent Holdings, China’s largest game publisher, and another to a subsidiary of China Ruyi Holdings, which is also backed by Tencent.
ByteDance had started to build a healthy cash flow for its AI business, even as its fourth-quarter profit declined, Chinese digital media outlet Jiemian reported on Friday, citing sources close to the company.
“We are proud of Moonton’s impressive growth into a leading mobile gaming player in Southeast Asia,” said a ByteDance representative. “This transaction marks a natural next step in its journey, and we look forward to seeing Moonton continue to push creative boundaries and inspire gaming communities around the world.”
Since its launch in 2016, Mobile Legends: Bang Bang had amassed more than 1.5 billion downloads, with a total monthly player base of more than 110 million, according to the Savvy representative.
The game is very popular in Southeast Asia. In Thailand, for example, it was the second most popular free app on Android devices as of Friday, according to app market intelligence Data.ai.
Riyadh-based Savvy said it highly valued the China market and its talent.
“China is a fantastic market because it’s a big one, and secondly, there’s some of the best talent in the world,” said CEO Brian Ward in an interview in November.

data.title: China’s Unitree Robotics rides humanoid tide as it targets US$610m IPO
data.description:
Unitree Robotics, a Hangzhou-based maker of quadruped and humanoid robots, has filed for an initial public offering (IPO) on Shanghai’s Star Market, seeking to raise 4.2 billion yuan (US$610 million) as revenue and profits surge on the back of rising interest in embodied artificial intelligence.
The listing application has been formally accepted by Shanghai’s exchange following a “preliminary review” on Friday.
The 10-year-old company recorded revenue of 1.71 billion yuan last year, up 335 per cent from a year earlier, while net profit on an adjusted basis rose nearly eight times to 600 million yuan, according to its prospectus published on Friday.
The IPO comes as embodied intelligence – the integration of AI with physical systems – becomes a global buzzword, particularly in China and the US.
Unitree said the listing would “strengthen capital resources and further enhance its end-to-end innovation capabilities and overall competitive edge in embodied intelligence”, with proceeds earmarked for developing robot bodies, AI models and manufacturing facilities.
Between 2022 and September 2025, Unitree shipped more than 30,000 quadruped robots, including enterprise and consumer-grade products. Over the same period, it delivered more than 4,000 humanoid robots, and the total for 2025 reached 5,500 units – ranking first globally, the company said, citing industry data.
Humanoid robots accounted for more than half of total revenue in the first nine months of last year, underscoring their growing importance to the business.
Revenue rose from 123 million yuan in 2022 to 159 million yuan in 2023 and 392 million yuan in 2024, before leaping in 2025. The company turned profitable in 2024, posting net income of 94.5 million yuan and 105 million yuan in 2025, compared with losses in the previous two years.
Net profit for 2025 reached 600 million yuan on an adjusted basis, up 674 per cent year on year, driven by a “sharp increase in product shipments”.

Gross margins in its core business, which includes robots and components, improved to 60.3 per cent in 2025, from 56.4 per cent in 2024 and 44.2 per cent in 2023.
Unitree said domestic sales accounted for most of its revenue for the first time in 2025, with overseas markets contributing 39.2 per cent.
The shift reflected “rapid development of China’s AI and robotics industries, alongside supportive policies, which have boosted demand for general-purpose robots across research, education, commercial and industrial applications”, the company said.
Its most recent funding round in June 2025 valued the company at 12.7 billion yuan.
Unitree’s profitability stands in contrast to Hong Kong-listed UBTech Robotics, which posted a net loss of 439 million yuan on revenue of 621 million yuan in the first half of 2025.
Unitree has attracted widespread attention over the past year, including after its robots danced on the Spring Festival Gala aired by national broadcaster CCTV. Founder Wang Xingxing, born in 1990, also attended a high-profile symposium chaired by President Xi Jinping in February 2025, where he was the youngest entrepreneur.
A wave of start-ups, including Galbot and Agibot, emerged in China in recent years and became investor darlings. But the sector remains under pressure to demonstrate practical value in industrial settings and ageing-care applications rather than eye-catching dance demonstrations.

data.title: Preview of Alibaba’s top AI model leads Chinese peers, lags US rivals
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding unveiled the preview version of its most powerful artificial intelligence model to date, solidifying its position as China’s leader in the race to catch American giants like Anthropic, Google and OpenAI.
Qwen3.5-Max-Preview, the flagship model of Alibaba’s Qwen 3.5 family, is now available on Arena, formerly known as LMArena, a model performance community created by researchers from UC Berkeley. Alibaba, which owns the South China Morning Post, unveiled the model on Thursday.
As of Friday, it was the top Chinese model on Arena’s list, but ranked 15th globally, behind products from Anthropic, which occupied the top two positions, and Google, whose Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview came third.
But Qwen3.5-Max-Preview shows strong maths capabilities, ranking fifth globally in the category, trailing models including Anthropic’s Claude-Opus-4-6-Thinking and OpenAI’s GPT-5.4-High.
Alibaba said the new model was currently undergoing “final optimisations ahead of the release within the next two weeks”.
The preview is the latest evolution of the Qwen 3.5 series, which Alibaba has been aggressively expanding. Last month, the Hangzhou, Zhejiang province-based company announced several models in the family. These included Qwen-3.5-Open-Source, with 397 billion parameters, and Qwen-3.5-Plus, which offers one of the industry’s largest context windows at 1 million tokens – a reflection of how much data it can process at any given time.
The release of Qwen3.5-Max-Preview followed Alibaba’s mixed earnings report on Thursday, where its revenue of 284.8 billion yuan (US$40.7 billion) for the December quarter, an increase of 2 per cent, missed analysts’ estimates.
Nevertheless, the firm aimed to generate US$100 billion in annual sales from its combined cloud and AI businesses within five years, executives said on a call with analysts on Thursday.
“Over the past three months, token consumption on the Model Studio platform has grown by six times,” said CEO Eddie Wu Yongming on the earnings call, adding that model-as-a-service offerings would become the cloud division’s largest growth driver.
Meanwhile, its T-Head unit, which focuses on chip design, shipped more than 470,000 AI chips as of February and was nearing 10 billion yuan in annual revenue over the past two years, the company said on Thursday.

data.title: Red faces at bureau after anti-drug advert sends wrong message
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Security Bureau has apologised for an advertising blunder in its anti-drug campaign, after a banner in a railway station appeared to encourage the use of illegal substances when viewed from certain angles.
The bureau addressed the issue in a social media post on Friday, a day after an internet user circulated a photo of the poorly placed advert at Tsim Sha Tsui MTR station.
The banner was part of the government’s latest anti-drug campaign, featuring one of Hong Kong’s “Four Heavenly Kings” of Cantopop, Aaron Kwok Fu-shing, and the slogan “Let’s stand firm together and not take drugs” written in Chinese.
In the photo, which went viral online on Thursday, some words in the slogan cannot be seen because of the angle, leaving the message: “Stand firm together and take drugs.”
Other internet users posted pictures of the advert being removed by workers by Thursday night.

The bureau acknowledged the mishap in its social media post but took the opportunity to reiterate its anti-drug message.
“We did not catch the angle in the ad, so we’ve taken it down immediately for a remake. This was a big lesson for us,” it said.
“If an ad is wrong, you can ‘take two’, but in life, if you take drugs, it is not that easy to turn over.”
Despite the apology, some internet users still criticised the bureau for the blunder.
“Remaking the advertisement inevitably means wasting more taxpayer money,” one Instagram user said.
Another internet user said: “The public relations team worked efficiently this time, but for those who approved the advertisement, how much are they being paid each month? Such a low-level mistake should not be made using taxpayer money; I suggest firing them.”

data.title: How China’s Hainan reinvented itself as Hong Kong’s rival – and partner
data.description:
As Hainan pushes ahead with its free-trade port, the tropical island is emerging as a potential rival to Hong Kong’s long-standing role as a regional shopping and low-tax hub – even as both sides signal interest in closer cooperation.
Since launching a separate customs regime three months ago, Hainan – now a gateway to the Chinese market – has positioned itself as a “super partner” to Hong Kong across industry chains, finance, tourism and other sectors, according to officials.
“Hong Kong serves as the mainland’s ‘superconnector’, and the Hainan-Hong Kong partnership can be fully upgraded to that of ‘super partners’,” said Wang Bin, chief of the Hainan provincial government’s publicity department, citing their “highly complementary advantages”.
Both economies – located in the South China Sea and close to Southeast Asian markets – offer corporate tax rates of 15 per cent. Long compared by market observers, they have now entered a phase of cooperative competition.
Wang said Hainan aimed to tap Hong Kong’s strengths in talent, legal services and finance, noting that the city already accounts for more than 70 per cent of the island province’s foreign investment.
He proposed a cross-border model of “orders in Hong Kong, production in Hainan and sales worldwide”, calling the island province an ideal hub to industrialise the city’s research capabilities.


Wang also called for leveraging Hong Kong’s status as a global financial hub to attract international capital and route more foreign tourists to Hainan via the city, highlighting the island’s edge in offshore bond issuance over other mainland provinces.
At the same event on Wednesday, Man Cheuk‑fei, vice-chairman of the Hong Kong Federation of Journalists, said Hong Kong firms – especially in finance and professional services – were well placed to expand into Hainan, citing opportunities in duty-free retail, cross-border trade, healthcare and aerospace.
As Hong Kong drafts its first five-year development plan, he added, cooperation projects with Hainan could be written into the blueprint.
Since the island-wide customs “closure” in mid-December, Hainan has rolled out a sweeping package of incentives, from expanded zero-tariff imports to duty-free access for locally processed goods under a separate customs regime.
The effect has been significant: zero-tariff items now account for about 74 per cent of goods on the island, while inbound visitors during the Spring Festival surged 82 per cent year on year, official data showed.
These measures are turning Hainan into a system that – with low taxes, freer capital flows and tariff-free trade – increasingly mirrors some of Hong Kong’s core economic functions. Still, Beijing has sought to cast the relationship as complementary.




Asked in January about potential competition, the Ministry of Commerce said Beijing would support Hong Kong’s role in the country’s broader development strategy and promote complementary development with Hainan.
Some in the business community also see room for a clear division of labour. Jonathan Choi Koon-shum, chairman of the Chinese General Chamber of Commerce in Hong Kong, said the city could leverage its strengths in modern services.
“We aim to empower Hainan with Hong Kong’s strengths in finance, law, accounting and so on, hoping to create a collaboration of complementary strengths,” said Choi, who is also a member of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference, during the “two sessions” earlier this month.
He is now advocating for Hong Kong’s legal system to be extended to Hainan’s arbitration framework.
“If English common law could be applied in Hainan, Hainan would develop even faster,” he said.

data.title: 5 jailed, 20 more arrested in Hong Kong crackdown on illegal employment
data.description:
Five people have been jailed and another 20 arrested as part of a Hong Kong Immigration Department crackdown on illegal employment.
The department said on Friday that it had raided 23 locations, including restaurants, cleaning companies and commercial buildings, during an operation carried out from Monday to Thursday.
Those arrested comprised 19 people found or suspected to be working illegally and six employers who allegedly hired them. The larger group consisted of six men and 13 women, aged 22 to 62.
Thirteen of those arrested held tourist visas – 11 from mainland China and two from Indonesia. A former domestic helper who allegedly overstayed, an illegal immigrant and four holders of recognisance forms were also arrested in the operation. A recognisance form is a temporary identification document that allows holders to remain in Hong Kong but not to work.
Ng Kwun-cheong, the deputy commander of an Immigration Department task force tackling illegal employment, said five people among those apprehended over illegal employment had already received prison sentences ranging from 51 days to 14 months.
“Most of the arrested illegal workers took the jobs, including dishwashers, cleaners and working in kitchens as general workers,” he said.
“Some of them had even worked for more than half a year.”

Ng said that most of those arrested were believed to have been paid a daily wage of HK$200 (US$26) to HK$600 in cash to avoid any checks.
The six employers arrested were all men, aged 39 to 53, and ran restaurants and cleaning companies targeted in the operation, he said.
Ng said the investigation was still ongoing and more people might be arrested.
“During the operation, we noticed that some illegal workers tried to forge copies of identity cards and used mobile phone screenshots to act as copies to seek jobs in an attempt to deceive the employers into believing that they could be legally employed,” he said.
Ng urged all employers to ask job applicants to present original identity documents for verification and to remain vigilant if candidates gave various reasons for only providing copies.
In Hong Kong, employers hiring workers illegally can receive a maximum fine of HK$500,000 and up to 10 years in jail.
Anyone caught violating their terms of stay in the city may also face prosecution. Those convicted face a fine of up to HK$50,000 and a maximum prison sentence of two years.

data.title: Global drug giants double down on China amid push for self-reliant supply chains
data.description:
AstraZeneca said it would establish drug manufacturing facilities in Guangzhou and Shanghai as well as a separate innovation centre in Shanghai, joining a group of foreign players building new plants in China amid geopolitical tensions.
The moves are part of a US$15 billion commitment the British firm pledged to make in the world’s second-largest drug market through 2030, unveiled during UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing in January.
China’s manufacturing capabilities, integrated supply chain and cost advantages were drawing multinationals to produce locally, said Zhang Jialin, head of China healthcare research at Nomura. “Beyond their long-term commitment to the China market, technical know-how and quality advantages are key considerations driving these companies to build here.”
On Thursday, AstraZeneca signed a memorandum of understanding with Guangzhou authorities to build a radioconjugate drug manufacturing site, which would produce next-generation cancer drugs for patients in China and the broader Asia-Pacific region, according to a statement on its website.
The new facility will be in the Guangzhou Economic and Technological Development Zone, a state-designated industrial district spanning 17.67 sq km in Huangpu district, east of the city centre. It will specialise in producing an actinium-225-based radioconjugate for prostate cancer treatment.

Separately, AstraZeneca said it planned to build a cell therapy production base in Shanghai’s Lingang Special Area, a government-designated free-trade zone designed to attract advanced manufacturing and hi-tech industries, as well as an innovation centre at Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park, a life sciences and tech hub in the Pudong district that is home to hundreds of pharmaceutical and biotech companies.
The Shanghai hub will manufacture and supply CAR-T cell therapies, a personalised cancer treatment engineered from a patient’s own immune cells, in China and other Asian markets.
The company said the Shanghai facilities would make it the first global biopharmaceutical firm with end-to-end cell therapy capabilities in China, covering early-stage research, viral vector and plasmid development, clinical manufacturing and regulatory support.
Also on Thursday, AstraZeneca joined hands with the Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission, the University of Glasgow, King’s College London and HSBC to launch the Shanghai-UK Life Science Innovation Ecosystem Initiative, aimed at linking Chinese innovation with global scientific and financial resources to accelerate healthcare development.
Radioconjugates, a transformative approach to cancer treatment, offered “more personalised and precise care while potentially improving on the outcomes of traditional radiotherapy”, Iskra Reic, AstraZeneca executive vice-president for international, said in the Chinese statement.
“The Greater Bay Area offers world-class manufacturing infrastructure and a mature biopharmaceutical ecosystem,” Reic added.
The Guangzhou base would work in tandem with a separate cell therapy manufacturing and innovation centre in Shanghai, according to the company.
The announcements come as AstraZeneca faces scrutiny in China. The company has been under investigation since last year over alleged irregularities involving the import of oncology drugs.
AstraZeneca is not alone in deepening its China manufacturing footprint. Eli Lilly this month announced a US$3 billion investment over the next decade to build a localised production and supply chain in China.
The centrepiece of the plan involves expanding its Suzhou facility and building high-volume manufacturing capacity for orforglipron, its first oral GLP-1 obesity drug currently under regulatory review in China.
Swiss drug maker Roche last year pledged 2.04 billion yuan (US$296 million) to build a second biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility at Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park, which will produce its eye drug Vabysmo and is expected to be completed in 2029.
Pharmaceutical giants were building self-reliant supply chains in Europe, the US, China and Japan, according to Tony Ren, head of Asia healthcare research at Macquarie Capital. “In case there is a pause in shipments between any of these two regions, the local production can meet local demand,” he said.
“US, China and Japan are the top three national pharmaceutical markets in the world. No rational [company] would want to decouple from any of them, despite political pressure,” he added.

data.title: Hong Kong crane collapse signals need for metal fatigue testing: expert
data.description:
Hong Kong should review its crane safety guidelines to include mandatory testing for metal fatigue, an industry expert has said after the city recorded another fatal case involving a collapse.
An operator was killed on Thursday afternoon when a tower crane fell at a construction site for the second phase of a public housing project on Tai Wo Hau Road, toppling onto a slope opposite Pik Kwai House at Kwai Chung Estate.
The project’s main contractor, Woon Lee Construction, was previously fined by authorities over two fatal incidents at its sites in 2009 and 2011.
Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, warned on Friday that the current mechanical inspections were insufficient.
Chau said it was his understanding that the tower crane had undergone a thorough inspection earlier this month and was subject to regular daily checks.
“However, the checks were primarily visual inspection or mechanical tests of cables, and they did not cover metal fatigue. It remains to be seen whether the current inspection scope is sufficient,” he said.
Chau said the crane at the site had only been in use for two to three years.
He noted that under Labour Department guidelines, only tower cranes used for more than 15 years needed to undergo non-destructive testing on bolts and non-welded sections.

The machines only required a weld test on 10 per cent of their joints, while metal fatigue was excluded from the inspection requirements, he said.
“A formal review of the guidelines is needed,” he said.
He said that the recent adoption of Modular Integrated Construction (MiC), in which pre-finished modules are built in factories and then assembled on site into complete buildings, might have significantly increased both the crane’s workload and its frequency of use.
Thursday’s crane collapse resulted in the operator, a 62-year-old man, falling onto the slope along with a 45-metre (148-foot) section of the machine. He was rescued from the mangled cabin with severe head injuries, but was pronounced dead at the scene.
The crane jib was still resting on the hillside as of Friday morning, with a section of Tai Wo Hau Road cordoned off.
The Housing Department earlier said that it would follow up on the incident and take appropriate action against the contractor based on the results of its investigation.
The department suspended all work at the site and met with the company to demand a detailed investigation report.
It added that it would pursue prosecutions if any violations were found.

data.title: Xiaomi’s new-generation SU7 launch sparks fresh EV fight with Tesla in China
data.description:
Xiaomi’s updated SU7 electric vehicle (EV) is expected to bite into the market share of Tesla and some domestic rivals in China, as demand weakens and regulators tighten oversight.
Founder and CEO Lei Jun on Thursday announced official pricing for the new-generation smart sedan, with the standard edition starting at 219,900 yuan (US$31,900) and the pro and max variants set at 249,900 yuan and 303,900 yuan, respectively.
The figures are 10,000 yuan lower than pre-sale estimates, but 4,000 yuan higher than the first-generation SU7, which launched in March 2024 at 215,900 yuan.
Lei said the adjustment reflected significant improvements in vehicle configurations and rising supply chain costs, adding that the new model offered strong value for money.
Citibank described the pricing as fair and suggested the model’s mature monthly run-rate could return to 270,000 units, according to a note on Thursday.
“When compared with competing brands in the same price range, it is roughly on par or slightly better than peers, without very obvious advantages beyond the average level,” said Citi analyst Kyna Wong. “We believe it will marginally take some of the Tesla Model 3, Zeekr 007 and Xpeng P7 market share.”

Xiaomi’s first-generation SU7 delivered more than 246,400 units in 2025, outselling the Tesla Model 3 to become the bestselling premium pure electric sedan priced above 200,000 yuan in China. Total deliveries reached 381,000 units before sales ended in February.
Morgan Stanley, in another report, said the update was poised for success given high consumer interest and tangible feature upgrades, believing that the improvements in “smart cabin experience and safety measures” strengthened its competitive edge.
The launch came as China’s new-energy vehicle sales fell 7 per cent year on year to 1.7 million units in the first two months of 2026, following the halving of a 10 per cent sales tax exemption.
Regulators renewed their push for stricter oversight of the EV market and urged manufacturers to shift focus from price wars to technological innovation in domestic automotive chips and self-driving systems.
The new SU7 featured a triple-redundancy door handle design that complied with 2027 national safety standards, ensuring emergency access even after a collision, Xiaomi said. The series now standardised Xiaomi’s hyper-autonomous driving technology, powered by its cognitive large model, supporting garage-level navigation and complex scenario reasoning, it added. Other enhancements included improved battery range, motor power and cabin noise control and sun protection.
The launch event drew industry leaders including BYD chairman Wang Chuanfu, Xpeng CEO He Xiaopeng and Li Auto CEO Li Xiang.
Xiaomi also revealed its long-term ambitions, including plans to invest 60 billion yuan in artificial intelligence over the next three years to further integrate its ecosystem.
Despite excitement for the product, Xiaomi’s shares had fallen nearly 7 per cent by the noon trading break in Hong Kong.

data.title: Panama⁠ accuses CK Hutchison subsidiary of lying about arbitration response
data.description:
Panamanian President Jose Raul Mulino has accused a subsidiary of Hong Kong conglomerate CK Hutchison Holdings of lying about the country’s response to an arbitration initiated by the company over the seizure of two strategic ports.
Mulino’s rebuttal on Thursday was in response to claims several days earlier by the Panama Ports Company (PPC) that his government had failed to file a response to the company’s request for arbitration by the deadline of March 13.
The company filed the request after the country seized control over terminals at the Balboa and Cristobal ports in February.
PPC, a subsidiary of the conglomerate owned by Hong Kong tycoon Li Ka-shing’s family, claimed the government had said it was “not prepared” and would not be able to respond in time because it had not hired any lawyers, “was not familiar with the dispute” and needed time to develop a plan.
Mulino reportedly rejected the accusation and said the government was backed by international counsel.
“[The accusation] is outrageous and a lie,” he said. “We have appointed ‌international lawyers who are going to defend us.”
The Panamanian government later issued a statement, accusing PPC and its affiliates of refusing to cooperate, concealing information and obstructing a coordinated transition.
It also described the company’s recent remarks as “fallacious and libellous”.
Panamanian authorities seized the Balboa and Cristobal ports in February, following a ruling by the country’s top court that declared the law approving the concession held by PPC to be unconstitutional.

The company described the seizure of the ports as an “illegal takeover”. PPC has operated the facilities since 1997 under a 25-year concession, renewed in 2021.
The canal, which carries about 5 per cent of global maritime trade, became a geopolitical flashpoint after United States President Donald Trump promised he would “take back” control of the waterway, which he accused of being under Beijing’s influence.
Speaking at the CK Hutchison results briefing on Thursday, group co-managing director Frank John Sixt said both the conglomerate and PPC were pursuing “all available legal remedies” through both national and international proceedings to resolve the dispute over the two Panama Canal ports.
He added that they were in discussions with the original consortium members, as well as a significant financial investor and a major strategic investor from China.
Sixt said the conglomerate was continuing to “work vigorously” with its legal advisers and would “provide updates when that is appropriate”.
The port dispute intersected with a separate attempt by CK Hutchison to sell more than 40 ports worldwide in a multibillion-dollar deal with a consortium led by US asset manager BlackRock and MSC.
Chinese regulators at the time signalled that they would scrutinise the transaction, and reports indicated Beijing sought a greater role for state-owned shipping giant Cosco in any arrangement involving the Panama terminals.
Without providing a reason, Cosco announced last week that it would suspend its operations at the Balboa port, prompting Panamanian authorities to publicly urge the shipping giant to reconsider its decision.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks waver as oil swings on Middle East tensions
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks swung between gains and losses on Friday as investors traded on headlines about the Middle East tensions and navigated volatile oil prices.
The Hang Seng Index fell 0.1 per cent to 25,471.66 as of 9.46am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 0.7 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index climbed 0.9 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index added 0.3 per cent.
Alibaba Group Holding tumbled 4.1 per cent to HK$126.50 after reporting a 67 per cent slump in quarterly earnings and sluggish revenue growth. Xiaomi sank 6 per cent after launching a new version of its electric vehicle.
Crude oil remained in focus as investors watched fuel prices for clues on whether the US-Israel war on Iran would thrust the world into stagflation or even a recession. Futures contracts on the fuel eased more than 2 per cent on Friday after Israel said it would no longer target energy infrastructure and Iran’s military capability had been degraded. Still, Brent oil traded above US$100 a barrel, a level unsettling investors over higher energy costs.
Other major Asian markets were mixed. South Korea’s Kospi rose 1.1 per cent, while Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 and Taiwan’s Taiex both lost 0.4 per cent. Japan’s market was closed for a holiday.
